title: Pinch-pot ring dish in air-dry clay
subtitle: A small open clay dish for rings and small jewellery
excerpt: A pinch-pot ring dish is a small, shallow bowl with a flat base formed from a single ball of clay. It takes under an hour to make and dries overnight. The wide opening keeps rings, earrings, and hairpins within reach. No kiln or wheel is needed. Air-dry clay handles decorative use without any sealing issues.
sourceNotes: Pinch-pot technique applied to jewellery storage form. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: ring dish is one of the fastest useful things to make from clay. A 100 g ball of clay, five minutes of pinching, and overnight drying produces a small dish that holds rings, earrings, and hairpins. Air-dry clay is not food-safe, but it is fully adequate for dry jewellery storage.

attrs.body: A 100 g ball makes a dish about 10 cm across and 2 cm deep, the right size for a bedside ring dish. A 150 g ball makes a slightly deeper bowl with more wall height. Stay within these weights for a first attempt: larger dishes need the walls supported as they dry or they sag.
attrs.title: Size guide

content.text: Roll 100 g of clay into a smooth ball. Press your thumb into the centre to within 8 mm of the base. Pinch between thumb inside and first two fingers outside, rotating the ball a quarter-turn after each pinch. For a ring dish the aim is a wide, flat opening rather than a tall vase form, so pinch outward as much as upward, spread the walls out from the base early.

Once the walls are about 3 cm tall, set the dish down on a flat surface and press the base firmly from outside with one finger to flatten it against the table. A flat base stops the dish rocking when in use. The walls should be 6 to 8 mm thick.

While the clay is still soft, press a stamp into the interior base of the dish or around the outer wall. Leaves pressed flat against the exterior surface transfer their veining cleanly. Keep the rim free of decoration so it stays smooth for items resting on the edge.
Step 4: Smooth the rim and dry
Run a damp finger around the rim to round and compress the edge. Leave on a flat surface to dry for 24 to 48 hours to
. Seal the exterior with two coats of PVA diluted 1:1 with water, or a matte acrylic varnish. Paint with acrylics first if a coloured finish is wanted, then seal.
Use the needle tool to write initials into the wet clay base for a personalised dish.
Fold one edge of the rim over slightly to create a lipped side that holds rings upright.

attrs.intro: Issues when making the ring dish.

items.fix: Dry slowly under a loose tent of plastic wrap for the first 12 hours. Check base thickness before drying: at least 8 mm throughout.
items.cause: Base was thinner than 8 mm, or the clay dried too fast.
items.symptom: Dish cracks around the base as it dries

items.fix: Press the thin sections gently between two fingers to even them out before the clay hardens. Trim any high spots with the needle tool.
items.symptom: Rim is uneven with thin and thick sections

attrs.heading: Common problems
